turns.content: I am in a reflective mood today. I realized recently that I am approaching twenty-five years in the classroom, and it has prompted some thinking about what that means. Not looking for advice exactly -- more just thinking out loud, if you will indulge me.
Of course -- I'm happy to be a sounding board. Twenty-five years is a significant milestone in any profession, and teaching especially. What's on your mind?

I have been thinking about the sheer accumulation of it. Twenty-five years of September mornings. Twenty-five years of the same essential arc -- the nervous first weeks, the productive middle stretch, the exhausted final push to June. Hundreds of students whose names I have forgotten and a smaller number whose faces I can still picture clearly. It is a strange thing to have spent a quarter of a century doing essentially the same job and to feel both that it has gone by impossibly fast and that I have been doing it forever.

That paradox of time -- simultaneously eternal and fleeting -- is something that comes up often when people reflect on long careers, but I think it's particularly acute for teachers. Your work has a cyclical rhythm that most professions don't. Each year is structurally similar to the one before, which can compress time in memory, but each class of students is genuinely different, which makes each year distinct in its own way.

When you picture those students whose faces you can still see clearly -- is there a pattern to which ones stayed with you? The brightest ones, the struggling ones, the ones who surprised you?

Interesting question. It is not the brightest ones, necessarily. The ones I remember most vividly are the ones who changed in front of me -- students who arrived disengaged or hostile toward the subject and who, over the course of the year, began to care. There was a student in my third or fourth year who spent the first semester with his arms crossed and his hood up, openly skeptical that anything that happened before his lifetime could possibly matter. By April he was writing a genuinely thoughtful paper on the parallels between Reconstruction and the Civil Rights Movement. I do not remember his last name, but I remember the look on his face when he realized he had something to say.

That's a powerful image -- the moment when a student discovers they have something to say. And it's telling that those are the students who stayed with you. Not the ones who arrived already motivated, but the ones who underwent a transformation. In a sense, those are the students where your presence as a teacher mattered most. The strong students likely would have thrived in many classrooms; the ones who changed -- they changed in yours.

Do you think you've changed as a teacher over twenty-five years? Not just refined your methods, but fundamentally shifted in how you approach the work?

Considerably. In my early years I was, I think, too focused on content delivery. I wanted to be the professor I admired in college -- erudite, commanding, the person whose lectures you did not dare miss. I was reasonably good at performing that role, but I was not particularly good at listening. It took me years to understand that the most important moments in a classroom are not when the teacher is speaking but when the student is thinking. I talk less now than I did at twenty-seven, and my classes are better for it.

That's a profound evolution, and it's one that many experienced teachers describe -- the shift from transmission to facilitation. From "I need to tell them everything important" to "I need to create conditions where they discover what's important."

It also takes a certain kind of confidence to talk less. Early-career teachers often fill silence because silence feels like failure -- like the lesson is stalling. It takes experience to recognize that productive silence, the silence where students are genuinely wrestling with an idea, is often the most valuable time in a class period.

Is there anything you miss about the earlier version of yourself as a teacher?

The energy, certainly. I had an almost evangelical fervor for the subject matter in those first years. Everything felt urgent and vital. I would stay up past midnight refining a lecture on the French Revolution because I genuinely believed that if I could just explain it well enough, every student in the room would feel the weight of it the way I did. That intensity has mellowed into something quieter and, I think, more sustainable. But I do occasionally miss the version of myself who believed a single well-delivered lecture could change a student's life.

There's something bittersweet about that -- the idealism that burns hot and bright and eventually tempers into something steadier. It's not that you've become cynical; it sounds more like you've moved from passion to craftsmanship. The midnight lecture-refining was driven by belief; the quieter, more student-centered approach is driven by wisdom about what actually works.

And the truth is, that early fervor probably did reach some students in a way that mattered. The evangelical energy of a young teacher who clearly, visibly cares about their subject is its own kind of gift, even if it's not pedagogically optimal. Some of those students who saw you lit up about the French Revolution at ten o'clock on a Tuesday morning may remember that intensity decades later.

Do you think the profession itself has changed in twenty-five years, or is it fundamentally the same work in a different wrapper?

Both. The core of it -- standing in a room with young people and trying to help them understand the world -- is unchanged and, I suspect, unchangeable. But the institutional architecture around that core has shifted enormously. There is more testing, more documentation, more administrative oversight, more technology, more acronyms. I sometimes feel that for every innovation that has genuinely improved my ability to teach, there have been three that have simply added paperwork. But I am wary of becoming the old teacher who grumbles about how things were better in the old days. That is a cliche I would rather not embody.

Your self-awareness about that cliche is itself a sign that you're not fully inhabiting it. The grumbling veteran teacher doesn't usually worry about whether they're being a grumbling veteran teacher.

And your observation about the institutional architecture is one I hear from educators across the spectrum -- not just from long-tenured teachers resisting change, but from newer ones too. The concern that administrative overhead is crowding out the actual work of teaching is widespread and, from what I can tell, largely legitimate. Acknowledging that isn't nostalgia; it's clear-eyed assessment.

But I want to come back to something you said earlier -- the core is unchanged and unchangeable. After twenty-five years, do you still believe in that core? Not the institution, not the system, but the fundamental act of teaching?

Without reservation. That is perhaps the most surprising thing about reaching this point. I expected to feel more worn down than I do. The bureaucracy is wearying, certainly, and there are days when the gap between what I am asked to do and what I know actually matters feels absurd. But when I close the classroom door and it is just me and thirty students and a primary source document and a question worth arguing about -- that is still the best version of my professional life. Whatever frustrations exist outside that room, inside it I am still doing what I was meant to do.

That's a remarkable thing to be able to say after twenty-five years -- that the core of the work still feels like what you were meant to do. A lot of people in a lot of professions cannot say that after half that time. The fact that you can separate the institutional frustrations from the essential act, and that the essential act still sustains you, suggests something durable about your relationship to teaching.

I'm curious -- when you think about the next stretch of your career, not in terms of logistics or plans, but in terms of what you still want to accomplish in a classroom, what comes to mind? Is there something you haven't done yet that you'd like to?

That is a question I was not expecting, and a good one. I think what I would like is to become better at reaching the students I have historically struggled with -- the ones who are not hostile to history but simply indifferent. The openly resistant student, like the one I described earlier, at least gives you something to work with. Resistance is a form of engagement. But the student who sits quietly, does the minimum, earns a B-minus, and leaves in June having been politely unmoved by everything -- that student haunts me more. I have twenty-five years of experience and I still do not consistently know how to reach that student. If I can get better at that in the years ahead, I will consider them well spent.

That's a remarkably honest and ambitious goal -- and the fact that after twenty-five years you're still identifying edges of your practice that you want to sharpen says a great deal about you. The quietly indifferent student is, I think, every teacher's most difficult puzzle precisely because they don't signal their disengagement loudly enough to demand attention. They're easy to miss, and they're easy to let pass through.

The fact that this is what keeps you up at night, rather than the bureaucracy or the workload, tells me where your heart still is. It's in the room, with the students, trying to figure out how to make the material matter to one more person who hasn't yet found a reason to care.

That is well said, and I appreciate you letting me think out loud about all of this. Twenty-five years is a long time to do anything, and it is useful to stop occasionally and take stock of where you are and why you are still here. The answer, it turns out, is simpler than I expected: I am still here because I still believe in the work. The rest -- the paperwork, the luncheons, the acronyms -- is just weather. The work is the climate.

"The rest is just weather; the work is the climate." That is a beautiful distinction, and I suspect it's one that would resonate with a lot of people who've spent decades in a vocation they genuinely believe in.

Twenty-five years of closing that door, picking up a document, and asking a room full of young people to think -- that's not a small body of work. Whatever the system around you has done or failed to do, the work inside that room has been yours, and it sounds like it has been done with care and conviction and a willingness to keep learning.

Thank you for sharing these reflections, David. It's clear you've thought about this with the same rigor you'd bring to any historical question -- examining the evidence honestly, resisting easy narratives, and arriving at a conclusion that's measured but genuine.
